data.title: Trump Was Right: August Jobs Show Powell Is 'Too Late'
data.description:
NEW YORK (September 5) - The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) disappointed with just 22,000 new jobs for August, according to the Establishment Survey, a collection of job creation data from businesses replying to the survey. The number was well below the consensus estimate of 75,000. The private payroll number was 38,000, after taking account of 16,000 government jobs lost. For the three months of the summer (June, July, and August), average jobs was just 29,000 for the three months, the lowest since the pandemic.

The BLS’s Household Survey, which polls the number of people taking jobs, and is viewed as eliminating workers holding more than one job, printed well above the Establishment Survey at 288,000 new jobs.

The unemployment rate and the U6 rate both ticked up to 4.3% from 4.2% and 8.1% from 7.9%, respectively. The “U6” is the percentage of the population that is unemployed, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force, plus total employed part time for economic reasons, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force. Some 436,000 people joined the labor force, pushing the Labor Force Participation Rate up slightly, to 62.3% from 62.2% in July.

Let’s look at our exclusive schedule of July and August Jobs Creation by Average Weekly Wages:

Jobs creation was led, once again, by what we call “government-adjacent” jobs: Private Education & Health Services and Healthcare and Social Services, which tend to be funded by governmental programs, followed by Leisure and Hospitality. We do not include “official” government jobs payrolls in our chart, but government lost 16,000 jobs.

ECONOMY GENERALLY

Today’s numbers with last month’s massive revisions have resuscitated our recession concerns that had troubled us going back to January. We had all but put them to rest based on the jobs reports that now seem to have been illusory.

As we explained back in January, historically, a recession is almost assured after the 3-month/10-year inverted yield curve is resolved. That had happened at the end of 2024.

The data of late does show the economy is slowing significantly. First quarter GDP printed basically down at -0.5% in the third and final estimate. So called “Real GDP” - GDP minus elements of “noise,” and formally referred to as “Real final sales to private domestic purchasers" - printed at 1.9% in the revised estimate. For 2025Q2, GDP printed at 3.3% in the Second Estimate released Augusts 28th. “Real final sales to private domestic purchasers,” printed at just 1.9%.

We have thought for some time that the threat to the economy from the tariffs is considerably overblown and largely driven by media hand-wringing. We continue to believe that seller margins, not revenue, will be affected by President Trump’s tariffs as we explained in this article from last November. As we explained just recently here, the main drivers of inflation are coming from services; not goods subject to tariffs and the decline in the US Dollar Index (DXY).

Nevertheless, we continue to believe the ongoing collapse of China’s manufacturing economy we discussed here will cause China’s prices for manufactured goods to fall so that the cost of Chinese goods, after tariffs, will be within a couple of standard deviations of the 2024 average prices to American consumers. Walmart (WMT) and, we expect a number of other large U.S. importers, will use the buyers market to force their wholesale price from Chinese manufacturers down considerably.

The public seems to think inflation expectations are overblown, with the 5 year inflation expectation basically at or around 2.32, about where it was when Trump was sworn in, but up around 30 bps from where it was in April, when the President's tariff policy was first announced.

It’s clear the Fed has botched rates for at least the last two meetings. There probably should have been quarter point cuts in both meetings, given the resolution of the 3 month/10 year inverted yield curve we discussed in January as an indicator of a recession. But the Fed may be forgiven given the garbage in the BLS jobs data for the past few months. Regular followers will know that we had allayed our concerns about recession back in January, given the apparently "great" jobs numbers that were later downgraded.

Perhaps the Fed will recognized the new circumstances and be pragmatic and cut rates 50 bps in this month’s meeting, but that’s probably unlikely from a political and PR perspective. The Fed wishes to maintain the aura of Fed “independence” against a president who has been highly critical of the Fed's rate targets. We're a little confused, though, by Beveridge Curve data that show job openings and unemployment closely aligned. We may do another piece on that anomaly next week as we fear it may cause the Fed to let rates "stand pat," given ongoing Fed speculation that "tariffs will cause inflation," a view we believe to be misplaced.

PROGNOSTICATIONS

The data point to a slowing economy. However, as we said in our Q2 2025 GDP report, we think there may be substantially increased capex, an element of the Gross Domestic Investment (GDI) category of GDP, owing to the Big Beautiful Bill. However, that won’t be reflected, likely, until at least the later part of September and will not be reflected in Q3 2025 GDP.

A bigger bump in GDI will come in Q4 2025 and in 2026. In view of today’s release, we are reducing our estimate of Q3 2025 to just 0.75%, +/- 25 bps. We expect the Fed will lower rates by only a quarter point in its September meeting.

The Fed should probably go the full 50 bps cut, in our view. The economy is worse off now than it was in September of last year when the Fed cut rates 50 bps. The GDP data for 2025Q1 and 2025Q2 has been illusory, whipsawed by net exports (NEX) as companies managed their tariff expectations in Q1 with lots of imports in 2025Q1. Those declined in 2025 Q2. (A negative net exports value (a trade deficit), like we had in Q1, subtracts from GDP. A positive NEX (a trade surplus) as we had in Q1 adds to GDP.)

Optional Other Data Points

These are data points we use to assess the overall standing of the economy and are provided here for information only.

GDP printed at 3.3% in Q2 2025, according to the Second Estimate released August 28th. Core GDP, or final sales to domestic purchasers, which eliminates some of the “noise” in the GDP report, like inventories and net exports, printed at 1.9%, down from 2.7% in the Q1 2025 and 3.0% in the preliminary estimate for Q2 2024.

PCE inflation for July increased 2.6 percent. Excluding food and energy, or Core PCE inflation, which is, reportedly, the Fed’s preferred measure of inflation, increased 2.9% from one year ago according to data released August 29th.

The Institute for Supply Management's Manufacturer's Purchasing Managers Index ((PMI)) printed at 48.7 for August, up 70 bps from July. The overall manufacturing sector has been declining for 6 months, albeit slower in August.

The August services Index grew to 52.0 from July’s 50.1

The Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) for July printed Wednesday, with 176,000 fewer job openings than in June, but with 41,000 more hires.

Privately-owned housing units authorized by building permits in July, released August 29th, were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1,354,000. This is 2.8 percent below the revised June rate of 1,393,000 and is 5.7 percent below the July 2024 rate of 1,436,000.

Personal income increased $112.3 billion (0.4 percent at a monthly rate) in July, according to estimates released August 29th by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. Disposable personal income (DPI)—personal income less personal current taxes—increased $93.9 billion (0.4 percent) and personal consumption expenditures (PCE) increased $108.9 billion (0.5 percent).

RealClearMarkets/TIPP Economic Optimism Index for September fell from 50.9 in August to 48.7, a 2.2-point decline (-4.3%).

_________________________________________

NOTE: Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/ geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe are underperforming. While we have never written about any companies that are our clients and do not include companies we write about here in our personal investment portfolios, we do provide here strategies that we would have recommended were the companies our clients. Others discuss new management strategies of non-client companies that we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions regarding such companies here, however, assume the company will not change.)

data.title: BMW Plus Qualcomm Equals The Automated Driving Play
data.description:
Introduction

I enjoy the kind of news like the one we just got from Qualcomm Incorporated (NASDAQ:QCOM) and BMW (OTC:BMWKY). I already picture a hall in Munich with the new BMW iX3 there, looking like no other car, doors closed, screens on, and all of this room smells like an expensive vehicle. This car is powered by a driver-assist system that was built for three years by BMW and Qualcomm, ready to help me with any of my needs. Dreaming is nice, but I am not in Munich and it’s not 2028 yet, so time to discuss what it means for both companies and for the Autonomous Driving as a sector.

It looks like the car industry has stopped waiting for humanity to switch to robotaxis that drive themselves everywhere and realized we can take this whole thing into a completely different direction, which potentially can be even more profitable. I am referring to the Level 2 Plus, which is already available and allowed by many regulators throughout the world. It’s not full self-driving, or FSD, but it can help the driver on long rides by handling the boring stuff and updating over time to get better. As you probably realized by now, Qualcomm makes the hardware and the software, and BMW is responsible for the car and the driving part.

What was announced

Qualcomm and BMW introduced Ride Pilot, which they have been working on for the past three years, and we can actually see that it’s not a raw agreement that was just signed. The company said that “System is validated in 60 countries worldwide and is targeted to be available in more than 100 countries by 2026,” which shows how big of progress has already been made. Regulators are always one of the biggest headwinds in this sector, so knowing they already have a huge market for dealerships is great.

They called this system Snapdragon Ride Pilot. The word “pilot” is there for a reason - this thing behaves like an actual, very capable co-pilot. On certain highways that are mapped and approved, it can keep the car in the lane, decide the speed, make safe lane changes, and even park itself. But the important part is that a human is still the driver. You can take off the hands sometimes, but the idea is that your eyes stay on the road, and you stay responsible. It’s advanced assistance that makes long, boring rides easier, but doesn't deliver you on its own.

A nice part is that this system isn’t limited to one country or a couple of streets, as I already said, there are already 60 countries, with a plan to reach 100. It means the hardware and software have been checked against local rules in all of those countries, road signs, streets, and driving conditions, so the feature can legally and safely turn on where it’s allowed.

It’s debuting in the all-new iX3 at IAA Mobility, and, importantly, Qualcomm is now offering this same system to any global automaker or Tier-1 supplier that wants it, which could prove to be a new powerful growth engine both for the company and its stock. Basically, now they turn a BMW partnership into a platform play for the rest of the industry.

BMW’s part

For BMW, the Neue Klasse is basically a fresh start. It’s the new way they’re building cars. The idea is that everything runs on software, and instead of having lots of little computers around the vehicle, it will have one strong central system. That’s what makes it possible to add things like hands-free highway driving or smooth lane changes. In the iX3, BMW calls these central units “superbrains,” and the one that controls the automated driving runs on Qualcomm’s Snapdragon Ride. They say it’s about twenty times more powerful than what they used before. For the company, it’s a chance to keep innovating and creating new features that will make them interesting again. While BMW, AUDI, and Mercedes are without a doubt great brands, newer, modern automakers, often Chinese electric manufacturers, catch the attention of the European crowd with electronics and new, cool features. Legacy brands need things like this not to lose their relevance.

Now, does it mean BMW will be discussed on the market as much as Xpeng (XPEV), Nio (NIO), BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), or Tesla (TSLA)? I don’t think so. It takes more than one partnership to change the narrative, but it will be worth following this company in the future and seeing maybe they have something more to offer.

Why Qualcomm wins here

For Qualcomm, at the same time, it’s a pretty big deal. This company spent years trying to move from phones into cars, first with infotainment and connectivity, then into safety-critical compute and an ADAS stack. And now, finally, this company will be able to present something physical you can touch and sit in.

What they’re rolling out is a system that sees the road mainly through cameras, with radar built in to back it up, which is tied into a safety framework that works across different countries. The software will keep learning through data collected from cars already on the road. The focus isn’t on some distant idea of fully driverless cars with no steering wheel, but on making Level 2 Plus work reliably across different markets. That’s also where most of the demand is today. And the fact that Qualcomm is offering Ride Pilot to other automakers right at launch shows the aggressive approach the company chose. It looks like they are ready to capture the market share.

How it compares

But not everything is perfect. This is a space with a lot of players already. Nvidia (NVDA) is training a very powerful, modular system, while Mobileye has been in the game the longest and is known for dependable safety features. Tesla and General Motors (GM) are building their own versions, too. What BMW and Qualcomm are doing looks more practical because they’re focusing on a Level 2 Plus system that’s built around safety, can be approved in many markets, and can grow with software updates, but still, there is no way of knowing yet if it will win the competition. In the end, the question is who can roll out hands-free driving that regulators will sign off on, drivers will actually trust, and automakers can put in cars across their lineups. On those points, Ride Pilot is starting from a strong position, but once again, any minor setback and this system can lose the competitive advantage forever.

The things to watch are:

  1. Regional feature maps: where hands-free is approved, how quickly BMW adds roads, and how they message the supervision requirement to avoid driver complacency.
  2. Packaging and price: whether this shows up standard on higher iX3 trims or as a subscription, and how that translates to take-rates.
  3. Expansion beyond BMW: how quickly do we see a second and third automaker adopt the same system, and at what sensor tier.

Besides, it’s very important whether the system keeps improving after people start driving it. The whole idea behind pairing BMW’s Neue Klasse with Ride Pilot is that the car shouldn’t feel outdated a year later, it should get smarter as time goes on. That comes down to how often the software gets updated and how smoothly those updates work. If everything just works without problems, then it’s doing what it’s supposed to, but we will be able to see that only after a decent amount of time passes.

Conclusion

I see this as the version of automated driving that actually has a chance to spread widely in the shorter term than a full autonomous vehicle. It’s basically a set of features you can already put in Berlin, Boston, or Tel Aviv without having many problems, and the demand for it promises to be decent. People are more and more into buying cars for software and electronics rather than engine qualities. The iX3 launch gives BMW a proper reset with modern computing power, and by opening the system to other automakers, Qualcomm is turning one product launch into a whole new business line.

When you look at the Qualcomm stock, the valuation adds weight to the case. Shares trade at forward earnings multiples well below the sector average, with a forward P/E around 13–15x compared to peers closer to 23–30x. Even on an EV/EBITDA basis, Qualcomm sits at a discount, the same as compared to its own 5-year average. The growth is pretty much still there, and the future looks interesting. To me, that combination makes Qualcomm a Buy at current levels. BMW will need to do better than one partnership to win a place in my portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AT&T: How The EchoStar Deal Is Different
data.description:
AT&T (NYSE:T) made an announcement that it closed a deal with EchoStar (SATS) to essentially acquire low-band and mid-band spectrum licenses for roughly $23 billion. The overall sales point is that this will provide future growth prospects for AT&T while allowing whole business growth. EchoStar, for its part under its "Boost Mobile" brand, will continue to operate to obtain customers that are not part of the AT&T marketing plan. This is sold as a "win-win" for both companies. This is a different strategy than the last one, which was presented to shareholders as accretive from the start.

Part of the reason for this is a brief reference during the conference call that the low band was not proceeding as planned (for EchoStar), which was concerning to the FTC. A little-noticed part of the deal (for the lower bands) is that AT&T has a plan to fix the situation after the deal closes, but it may take a few years, as AT&T itself does not have much of a presence in that area of the market. It is also likely something that has to be approved by the regulatory bodies, as AT&T is buying something that would be lost through noncompliance.

The strategy for this deal is similar to the one detail in my coverage of Chart Industries (GTLS) (which is now going to be acquired by Baker Hughes (BKR)). In the 40-plus years that I covered Chart Industries, it grew by becoming a "one-stop shop" for more and more customers. The idea behind acquisitions was to accretively attract sales that the two parts could not accomplish separately. The result was the company grew rapidly through these types of acquisitions, as I documented through articles over the years.

For AT&T, first of all, the company is far larger, and secondly, this is the first transaction in this direction since the widely panned "shopping spree" that ended up largely creating the company as it now exists. This is one of those deals where the market hears the story and then goes "yah yah," followed by a yawn.

The reason is that in terms of strategy, a whole lot of companies do this, and then the market ends up asking, "where are the results?" It does not help that management stated it would take about 36 months for the transaction to become accretive. But this is a situation where the company is acquiring existing expansion capacity from someone that either does not need it or cannot do what is necessary to comfortably maintain the licenses. Therefore, this purchase does not fill up "the day after" the transaction closes. Instead, it will take some time for this to happen.

The other thing to consider is that this transaction expands an existing business with a little bit in a new area, whereas before it was a "shopping spree" of all kinds of new businesses that the company thought were needed for it to survive in the future. Here, the company is buying essentially a raw material (if you will) that it knows very well what to do with, as it is doing it in other areas of the business. As long as there is not another shopping spree of like transactions, management is unlikely to be overwhelmed with acquisition-type expenditures and growth plans that would lead to another borrowing binge.

Furthermore, there is a plan to get the debt ratio back down within roughly 36 months after the transaction closes. That is after considering that a debt ratio in the 3 range is probably nothing to be concerned about in the first place. Instead, it is more about getting that debt ratio lower so that the company is in a position to take advantage of deals as they appear.

That means this one deal is not a "company wrecker" if it fails. In fact, it probably has more than an average chance to succeed. By all means, management should be held accountable for the progress of the pending deals and then the accuracy of the "sales pitch" after the deal closes. But there is no need to panic that the company is "throwing money away" based upon the fears of what happened before.

Last Article

My last article noted that the income statement was cleaning up with the final significant divestiture of the DirecTV holdings announced. But this announcement, combined with some previous announcements, will include some nonrecurring costs when these various deals close.

These nonrecurring costs should not last nearly as long as it took to clean up earnings from all the divestitures and refocusing that took place.

The other thing is that debt is going to climb again. The difference is that one deal is supposedly accretive the day it closes, while this one will benefit the company in the form of more growth opportunities. Also, the value of the combined deals is less than $30 billion (without considering adjustments).

That combined value is far less than what happened during the "shopping spree." That means there are a lot of differences between the latest deals compared to what the market fears from the past. But it also means that management has to prove what it says are the goals. I personally like management's chances. Now let us see what happens.

Summary

AT&T seems to have a deal that has darn good chances to succeed. Management strategy books like Michael Porter's "Competitive Strategies" detail what it takes for a business combination to succeed. Forays into new businesses are obviously the riskiest (and for good reason). But this appears to be a well-thought-out expansion of offerings into areas that might have been half-served or avoided altogether.

In sum, the latest strategic announcements appear to be acquisitions that will profitably expand the business. In any event, none are so large that they are "bet the company" transactions.

Still, it may be time to lower my rating from a Strong Buy to a Buy. I still believe that the stock is a good deal. But the stock price reaction to the announcement may mean that the bargain that existed a while back is slowly disappearing. The price-earnings ratio is fine for those that want to get in, and the dividend yield is down from past highs but still reasonable. This is still a good play as a large company growth and income idea. I just think the recovery potential that existed for some time is now largely gone, and so it is time to reduce the rating. But for what the company appears to offer going forward, the current price is reasonable.

Risks

Third quarter earnings are due out on October 22. This is the first chance that management will have to report any unfavorable changes to the announced deals. Deals can come undone for any number of foreseen and unforeseen events. It is unlikely if the proper due diligence was done before the deal was announced. But it can happen.

Any acquisition can fail to meet future expectations. This risk is minimized in that the company is acquiring assets that it knows very well what to do with those assets once they are acquired. This is very different from acquiring new businesses.

Right now, there exists a fear of economic uncertainty. The business here is fairly recession resistant. However, a severe and sustained economic downturn could materially alter the outlook of the company.

data.title: New Gold: Still A Buy Despite The Recent Surge
data.description:
Introduction

Since my article titled New Gold: Potentially Perfect Timing Makes It A Buy was published, New Gold (NYSE:NGD) is up significantly. However, the key thing to remember here is that the stock’s performance had very little to do with the company’s recent developments, which I’ll cover below, with the stock remaining almost flat on earnings day and even a bit after it.

Why It Surged

The stock is up about 35% since I called it a Buy. As I said in the analysis, rate cuts will be a key catalyst for gold and gold miners, and we can see a majority of them up significantly since August, with the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) being up ~25.5% since the end of July. This, combined with trade deals, renewed threats, recession fears, and even seeing tariffs being ruled illegal by a federal appeals court a few days ago (which could lead to the US even having to refund $200 billion to businesses), led to what the market hates a lot: uncertainty—which is amazing for gold, as we can see (the commodity is up 8.4% since the end of July to a new all-time record).

Especially following Powell’s Jackson Hole remarks, the market sees rate cuts as something that’s significantly more likely to happen in the near future, which will have a clear effect on the economy, the stock market, gold, and more. As of a few days ago, the markets were pricing in a nearly 92% chance of a 25 basis points cut at the Fed's September 17 meeting, so we’re talking about very high expectations.

To be fair, I think this is more about getting a more tangible date and a reconfirmed focus on rate cuts rather than expecting them “in the future”—leading ”to less uncertainty around a catalyst that’s potentially very beneficial for gold prices, alongside many other commodities. Rate cuts would lead to lower treasury yields, which means a lower opportunity cost for holding gold,

At the same time, fear around an “accidental hard landing” is rising due to potentially weaker jobs data, rising consumer inflation, and a delayed effect of tariffs on prices fueled (and inflation) fueling concerns. This uncertainty is once again great for gold thanks to its status, while rate cuts can lead to lower treasury yields and a weaker USD due to money chasing better yields.

However, I’d like to reiterate that this is a general industry thing, not a company-specific one, and the potential of the initial thesis remains unchanged—or actually enhanced if gold prices remain high.

The Earnings Report

The company’s Q2 production and costs were as expected, with production of approximately 78,600 oz of gold and 13.5 million lb of copper. As they say, this increase in gold production compared to Q2’24 has been driven by the planned higher feed grade at Rainy River, partially offset by lower planned feed grade at New Afton. The AISC for the quarter was $1,393 per ounce, similar to Q2 last year and much better than Q1 2025, and what’s even better is that the company expects it to continue to go down throughout the year as production increases, which is part of their potential.

Also worth noting is that the overall gold production in H1 was ~38% of the midpoint of the consolidated production guidance of 325,000 to 365,000 oz, consistent with the planned 38% stated in the February outlook, so the company has been almost perfectly on point with this, and the reliability of their projections and their ability to execute on them going forward can lead to massive developments, as I’ll mention below.

The New Afton mine is expected to continue producing 60,000 to 70,000 ounces of gold and 50,000 to 60,000 lb of copper in 2025, which is in line with 2024. However, this was achieved at no cost while the company made some improvements for the mine, with mine life expected to be pushed for one more year (until 2031) and potential for more.

At Rainy River, we see a similar story, with lower production in H1 that will be ramped up to potentially reach an expected 265,000 to 295,000 ounces vs. ~225,700 ounces in 2024, but it’s also worth noting that Rainy River experienced operational challenges in December that led to them missing its gold production guidance for the year (between 230,000 and 240,000 ounces for this mine). However, the company is expanding the mine’s open-pit operations thanks to Phase 5, which should expand its lifetime from the initial 2028 depletion date, as well as a focus on developing its underground operations, which comes with the breakthrough of the underground ramp to the pit portal announced in April and the ventilation loop completed in late June. These are key steps that should allow the company to boost production from several new mining zones, expected to come online in 2025.

Reiterating My Thesis

Given the company’s growth potential, we’re likely looking at a free cash flow of as much as $1 billion in 2026, which is still amazing for a $4.8 billion market cap. However, I believe that currently they aren’t trading at the same valuations as most other peers because of the short life of mines, even though the company will have a significant boost of capital over the next couple of years.

As they say, 2024 and especially 2025 should be significant inflection points for the free cash flow, especially thanks to gold now reaching new all-time records. As I said in the analysis, we might be looking at potentially perfect timing from the company in many aspects.

First, we have the 19.9% free cash flow interest acquisition in New Afton, allowing the company to have full ownership now, which was done back in April, potentially adding significant cash flows going forward. On top of this, we have what they called “exceptional” drill results back in September 2024, which could boost the cash flows for the long term.

And that’s where the big risk comes into play. Yes, the company is expecting massive drops in costs and CAPEX after 2025 since they’re finishing these ramp-ups, allowing them to enjoy potentially phenomenal 2026 and 2027 numbers if gold prices remain high. However, what happens after? Sure, there’s underground potential in Rainy River and New Afton can run, especially as a copper operation in the longer term, but that’s uncertainty, which the market doesn’t like, as we know.

They’ll gain a massive amount of cash flow that’s more than enough to do very significant acquisitions and expand production through other mines for the long term, but what if they decide to spend most of that money on dividends, or the project/projects they buy turn out to be a failure, as we’ve seen happening, especially for smaller miners, before? This is what keeps me from rating them a Strong Buy and only a normal Buy even now, as the management’s decisions can make the difference between an amazing long-term potential and a mediocre one.

And relying on two mines is also a problem, as we’ve seen even back in December 2024 with the operational challenges mentioned at their Rainy River mine. This time, it was only some unexpected mechanical downtime on the crushing and conveying system, resulting in lower-than-expected throughput at the mill, but what if the next issue will be much more significant? They not only rely on two mines, but Rainy River accounts for about 77% of their production, so of course, the market takes this into account when valuing the company.

Valuation

However, I believe that the current price is still far from the company’s potential if things go okay and they manage to offset the declining mines through acquisitions. Despite gold’s surge to all-time highs, I’ll keep the valuation unchanged to be more conservative, even though I remain bullish on gold. Any jump above the initial estimate will be a very welcome bonus.

As you can see from the updated valuation that includes their latest numbers (while we keep the assumption for gold at $3,250/oz, assuming that it falls from the current record level over this time), if they reach their 2025, 2026, and 2027 targets (so far, so good based on H1 2025) and account for a massive 8% FCF decline rate from 2028 to 2030, after which they remain flat and only assume a 2% terminal growth rate with a 9% WACC, we’re still potentially looking at a good return even after the recent jump. The key factor here will be proving their potential and how they'll utilize the cash flows that should come over the following years. If they do this and announce some merger, acquisition, or some developments that can at least flatline that production from 2030 onward after the decline we assumed, this stock and the company have significant potential.

Conclusions

Overall, New Gold remains a Buy in my opinion, thanks to the massive potential they have, thanks to the cash flows that should come over the coming few years, with a good balance sheet and significant upside potential. However, the company’s reliance on two mines and uncertainty around how the management will address their declining production keep me from rating it a Strong Buy, as their future will depend significantly on the management’s decisions.

data.title: Broadcom: Strong Buy On AI Supercycle And Software
data.description:
Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) has been one of the companies I have been extremely bullish on that paid off with a 68% increase in the company’s stock price, sharply outperforming the S&P 500’s 21% gain on AI strength. In this report, I discuss the Q3 2025 results, the guidance for Q4 and beyond, and update my price target.

Broadcom Stock Defies Trade Turmoil

In my prior report, I maintained my "Buy" rating but lowered my price target on expectations that the market would await some clarity on the trade picture. That clarity is now here with the United States and China having a truce in the trade war. To some extent, that removes any sentiment overhang on the stock price. We also note that we have seen no slowing down on CapEx supporting AI and data center expansion. So, the macroeconomic uncertainty is not driving down CapEx, it seems, and that is likely driven by the fact that most companies attempting to capitalize on AI realize that near-term uncertainty does not materially affect the long-term capacity requirements to facilitate deeper AI adoption.

Earnings Estimates Have Increased For Broadcom

Revenues during the third quarter grew by 22% to $15.95 billion. Semiconductor sales grew 26% to $9.17 billion, driven by AI revenues climbing 63% to $5.2 billion, driven by higher XPU sales. The non-AI part of the semiconductor business was flat sequentially, just like wireless and industrial sales. Enterprise networking and server storage sales were down, while broadband solution sales were up. The software segment grew 17% year-over-year.

It was a good quarter, with AI and VMware leading the growth and driving 30% growth in EBITDA as margins improved from 62.9% to 67%, above the 67% that the company had guided for.

A Good Quarter And An Even Nicer Outlook

The Q3 results were good. However, what I liked even better was the outlook. For Q4, the company expects AI revenues to grow to $6.2 billion, marking a 66% year-over-year increase, and non-AI semiconductor sales of $4.6 billion. In combination with VMware, the company expects sales of $17.4 billion, up 24% year-on-year with 9.1% sequential growth. EBITDA margins are expected to be 67%.

The company also announced a surprise $10 billion order from a major customer, which will drive the outlook for 2026, as discussed during the Q3 2025 Broadcom earnings call:

Now further to these 3 customers, as we had previously mentioned, we have been working with other prospects on their own AI accelerators. Last quarter, one of these prospects released production orders to Broadcom, and we have accordingly characterized them as a qualified customer for XPUs and, in fact, have secured over $10 billion of orders of AI racks based on our XPUs. And reflecting this, we now expect the outlook for our fiscal 2026 AI revenue to improve significantly from what we had indicated last quarter.

The company earlier indicated 50%-60% revenue growth in its AI business. With this latest deal, we see a huge sign of confidence in demand for AI and subsequent accelerations, and that drives Broadcom to expect AI revenue growth to accelerate in fiscal year 2026.

The company has a bit of a challenged non-AI semiconductor business, but VMware and AI are growing nicely with growth acceleration and margin expansion in prospect.

Broadcom Valuation Reflects Software And AI Strength

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Based on the median company EV/EBITDA, I am adjusting my price target to $414 per share. This is driven by a higher expected EBITDA. In 2025, this increase is still modest at 1.9%, but in the years after, the EBITDA estimates have been adjusted upward by 10.4% for 2026 and 11.6% for 2027, driven by the big AI deal that Broadcom announced. CAGR has improved from 20.6% to 25.1% with an 8.5% increase in total EBITDA generation. For free cash flow, there is a 6% downward revision for 2025, but 3% up in 2026 and 12.4% in 2027. This drove the CAGR from 31.5% to 36.4%, with a total upward revision in free cash flow generation of 4.2%. All of this also paves the way for continued and higher share repurchases, as we note that the company will have extremely low leverage.

Conclusion: Broadcom Is Positioned Well On Two Growth Drivers

I believe Broadcom is uniquely positioned for long-term value generation. The first growth driver is AI, where Broadcom can provide AI accelerators. Currently, it appears that the hyperscalers are not slowing down capital expenditures, and that provides a multi-year path for growth for Broadcom. The other growth driver is VMware. Broadcom recently launched its VMware cloud solution, and we see that Broadcom is really growing that part of the business at a high margin. As a result, I believe the stock is a "Strong Buy."

data.title: Apple: Sell While Ahead - Weak AI And Mature Markets Signal Caution
data.description:
When investing in companies, we are usually willing to overpay for their stocks when we hold a general conviction that its future earnings growth justifies current premiums. However, when the prime narrative being pushed by the company's management does not exactly demonstrate much growth potential, the price becomes really hard to defend.

This pretty much sums up my view on Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL). The company has, very noticeably, been lagging behind in the AI proliferation boom, while other tech giants have been aggressively expanding their capabilities and experiencing unprecedented growth as a result. Apple's AI initiatives, on the other hand, have come across as underwhelming and more incremental than revolutionary. Its strategy seems to suggest a defensive posture rather than a clear strategy to capture new growth opportunities.

For a company with a highly mature core business and saturated key markets, this spells trouble, especially given its high multiples. Its current pricing does not justify the growth it can still capture.

Interestingly enough, this view seems to tie in with Warren Buffett's selling off of his stake in Apple in recent years. I believe these moves are a clear signal that cashing in on profits may be the most rational step to take at current levels.

I rate AAPL stock as a Sell.

Valuation Assessment

At its ~$240 price tag, Apple holds a market capitalization of nearly $3.6 trillion. Given its trailing-twelve month [TTM] net income of $99.3 billion, the stock is trading with a TTM P/E [GAAP] ratio of 36.5x. This is about 26% higher than the tech sector median PE ratio of 23.3x.

This, of course, is an interesting discrepancy, considering that, over the next 12 months, Apple is expected to see its bottom line climb by 9%, which is actually below the median EPS growth expectation in the wider sector of 11%. This sub-sector growth expectation, despite a noticeable premium to the median multiple figures, suggests that investors are paying more to get their hands on AAPL, only to achieve lower growth. Clearly, this doesn't exactly suggest much of a rational trade-off.

In fact, looking historically, over the last 10 years, we see a clear multiple expansion with the stock's P/E ratio, coinciding with a general decline in earnings growth:

The more this gap widens, the less the stock's premiums stand as being justified. The only way these multiples would make sense, however, would be if the Apple growth narrative dramatically shifted in a way that reignites genuine excitement among bulls. This, of course, would mean growth far beyond its core business (which is already in a highly mature state, with its key markets being highly saturated) and possibly even a disruptive new product category or platform.

Judging by the management's commentary in the company's recent earnings call, it is evident that they are framing Apple's future narrative around deeper AI integration. However, as I will elaborate in the next section, I believe this framing reflects a defensive posture rather than providing any evidence of a true growth engine.

Apple’s AI Lag and Strategic Implications

It's pretty well known among investor circles that Apple has been quite behind on the AI front. As a tech titan in the Magnificent Seven category, the company's cautious approach has been quite noticeable and has certainly raised eyebrows. Despite incremental efforts to integrate AI into its existing offerings (such as its Genmoji update, Image Wand, and Visual Intelligence with Camera Control, etc.), Apple didn't exactly "wow" the market as it did with previous breakthroughs and platform innovations. In fact, even as feature upgrades, the advancements seem more defensive, rather than the proactive launches we've seen during the golden Jobs era.

This, of course, is understandable, considering that so many big tech players have been racing to showcase their AI game changers, and Apple can’t afford the optics of silence. It also risks seeing its tech being outdated without layering AI into its ecosystem.

Recently, the company has been in the spotlight, following reports that it is in talks with Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) regarding the use of the Gemini model to power an AI update to the Siri assistant (expected to launch next year). To me, this move seems more of a win for Google than it does for Apple. For one, the nature of the deal makes it seem like Apple is defensively playing catch-up rather than steering AI capabilities in a direction that actually expands its moat. On the other hand, Google benefits financially from the licensing of its AI model being deployed to Apple's colossal user base.

From a strategic perspective, this raises two issues for Apple, in my humble opinion. Firstly, there is a major dependence here on Google's underlying AI capabilities. While Apple has, for years, built upon this narrative that it boasts full control of its ecosystem, from hardware and software to its high-margin services business, ceding control of the AI layer to Google, a tech titan that many consider a rival to Apple, makes for poor optics. Even beyond optics, the dynamic could cause problems considering potential disagreements between Google and Apple on privacy-related matters.

Secondly, it is still unclear how the AI enhancements to Siri will actually deliver enough of a growth upside to justify current premiums seen in the price multiples. Generative AI is already widely available across a range of platforms such as Google, Microsoft, OpenAI, Meta, etc. I do not see how Apple's adoption of Gemini creates a unique growth lever. Coming onto the scene with "another ChatGPT/Google Assistant" with Apple branding is far from a reason to see a switchover towards the Apple ecosystem or a TAM expansion.

Overall, I believe the move to be pragmatic for Apple, but it does not really guarantee meaningful growth.

Coupled with the high valuation we see above, I believe that now is a good time for long-time holders to cash in on their profits and reallocate to higher-growth assets.

As validation of this position, I believe it really isn't a coincidence that Warren Buffett, one of Apple’s most prominent and vocal backers, has been trimming his position in the company since late 2023. Buffett started loading up on AAPL stock early in 2016, and it had accounted for nearly half of Berkshire Hathaway Inc.'s (BRK.A) (BRK.B) equity portfolio by 2023. By the third quarter of 2024, nearly two-thirds of the stake had been sold for cash. Furthermore, it was revealed recently, according to an SEC filing, that in Q2 of this year, Berkshire Hathaway offloaded an additional 20 million Apple shares.

In my opinion, Buffett's actions speak far louder than any narrative Apple may attempt to latch onto. Berkshire Hathaway has been locked in massive profits on the stock since 2016, but once the premiums began escaping fundamental reality and the growth trajectory no longer seemed like it could be sustained to the level of the company's glory days, cashing in became the most rational choice.

In light of these facts, I am reiterating my sell stance on Apple.

Risk to Thesis

Despite my strong beliefs that selling Apple is the right thing to do for shareholders in the company, it could very well be premature to underestimate how robust the company's control over its ecosystem is. There are a few companies out there that boast such a high loyalty and sticky user base, given the high quality of service across hardware, software, and services.

To assume that Apple will never introduce a game-changing AI-based offering that both reinforces and leverages its ecosystem might be a mistake. The company has a strong track record of proving the market wrong and the resources to invest heavily.

Even Berkshire Hathaway continues to hold a stake in the company, perhaps for the optionality it provides and the possibility that Apple could still deliver a breakthrough capable of driving massive growth across its ecosystem.

Investor Takeaway

Apple's growth narrative is under threat. The company faces a highly mature core business and saturated key markets, and above all, the risk of its ecosystem being outdated without strategically layering in AI into its offerings.

Apple has noticeably been lagging behind other tech titans in the AI race, focusing primarily on incremental initiatives that seem more defensive rather than transformative.

This is a problem for the shareholders of the company, given the stock's lofty premiums, which assume high growth to come. Since I do not believe current AI partnerships meaningfully contribute to growth of such proportions, I am rating the stock as a Sell.

data.title: Honeywell: Attractively Valued With Potential Upside Post Breakup
data.description:
Recap

In January of this year, I provided an update on Honeywell International Inc. (NASDAQ:HON). At the time, the stock was trading for a marginal discount to fair value and presented an enticing long-term forward return above 10%. However, my ultimate rating for the stock was a Hold, largely based on the uncertainty surrounding the rumored breakup of the company following pressure from activist investor Elliot Investment Management. Much has happened since January, so I thought it would be a good idea to revisit my investment thesis and discuss some key developments.

The Breakup

Shortly after my article was published, on February 6th, 2025, Honeywell announced that it will restructure into three standalone companies.

  • Honeywell Automation
  • Honeywell Aerospace
  • Honeywell Advanced Materials.

The rationale for this decision provided by management was better strategic focus, financial flexibility, and shareholder value.

Each new standalone company will be able to solely concentrate on its core market and products. Independent financials will allow for more tailored capital reinvestment without the burden of oversight from a conglomerate vision. Each company will be easier to value as a pure-play business that's not bogged down by a high-level unified vision. Shareholders will be allowed more flexibility to allocate capital in the pursuit of Aerospace, Automation or Advanced Materials without the burden of having to own all or nothing.

Honeywell Automation will focus on industrial automation, building controls, and safety systems. Honeywell Aerospace will focus on aviation, defense, and space. And Honeywell Advanced Materials will focus on high-performance materials; it will also include the Solstice product line that I will touch on later.

The Advanced Materials spinoff is planned to be the first step in this breakup, with the projected timeline being late 2025/early 2026. The Automation and Aerospace separation is expected to take place in the second half of 2026. More recently, the company also announced that it is exploring divestitures of its Productivity Solutions and Services Warehouse and Workflow Solutions. It's worthy to note that each business line generates roughly $1B in sales at the moment.

Current shareholders will receive shares in the new companies proportionally, and management has structured this transition to be tax-efficient. The company will leverage Section 355 of the U.S. Internal Revenue Code to make the separation a tax-free spin-off. Each shareholder will receive a proportional amount of shares of each new company, with their original cost basis also proportionally allocated across HON and the new companies.

The dividend policy for each new entity is still to be determined, but management indicated that all three new companies are expected to maintain strong dividend frameworks.

Honeywell Aerospace

The aerospace segment is the largest contributor to Honeywell's operating profit. In recently announced Q2 earnings, revenue for this segment went up 11% year-over-year, with 6% of the growth being organic. Segment margins contracted by 170 bps largely due to inflation and supply chain pressure.

40% of revenue comes from parts and services that are resilient to cyclical aircraft sales. The company is well diversified between servicing commercial, business, military, and space industries. With operations spanning across Asia, Europe, and North America, Honeywell has a wide reach, which gives the company a strong moat around its business lines.

Honeywell Automation

The automation segment is the second largest contributor to Honeywell's operating profit. In the latest earnings release, sales saw a slight decline year-over-year, roughly 5%, explained by cyclical industrial demand. Segment margins held steady at 19.2%.

This business line enjoys steady recurring revenue from subscription-based control and monitoring solutions. Products and services are sold to multiple industries, offering some resilience from cyclical downturns. The company also has a global footprint in this segment and differentiates itself from competitors by offering connected systems and leveraging IIoT analytics.

Honeywell Advanced Materials

The Advanced Materials is a much smaller segment of Honeywell, but by no means one to overlook. The segment has seen strong growth from its green-friendly products, largely Solstice, which could be a catalyst for continued future growth.

Solstice is Honeywell's branded line of environmentally friendly refrigerants. The Solstice product line has been a key revenue driver for the company, and it will give the Advanced Materials company a strong standalone financial profile. It's not a secret that regulatory trends are shifting towards more eco-friendly policies, and decarbonization is becoming a priority for most industries. Solstice will help Advanced Materials capitalize on this shift, and shareholders will have direct exposure to this high-growth, specialized chemical business.

The recent acquisition announcement of Johnson Matthey's catalyst business is another positive for Advanced Materials. The specialty chemical company based in the UK will strengthen Advanced Materials footprint in the high-margin chemical market. The deal is valued at roughly $1.8B and expected to close in early 2026.

As a standalone business, Advanced Materials, as well as the Automation and Aerospace companies, stands to see improved valuations relative to Honeywell. Pure-play companies in each of these industries have historically traded for much more generous multiples relative to conglomerates.

Honeywell International

And of course Honeywell is not going away post-breakup. Shareholders will not only receive shares in each of the three newly formed entities, but they will also retain ownership of what's left over at Honeywell.

Post breakup, Honeywell will be a much leaner business with the focus directed towards automation technologies, safety, energy efficiency, and connected business and industrial solutions.

An interesting development for Honeywell is its investment in Quantinuum, a quantum computing company that was formed as a joint venture with Cambridge Quantum. Quantinuum is currently one of the largest integrated quantum computing companies in the world, and Honeywell is a majority shareholder. The business delves into hardware, software, builds algorithms, and extends into cybersecurity. In 2023 the company released its H2 quantum computer that is one of the most powerful trapped ion systems. They are a pioneer in the work of applying quantum algorithms to language and AI. And have had notable uses in the fields of science and drug discovery research.

The company is preparing to go public, with the initial public offering expected in late 2026 or 2027. Honeywell intends to retain its majority stake through the upcoming IPO and capitalize on the future of quantum computing.

Valuation

Let's take a quick look at how attractively valued Honeywell is right now, and then I'll share my thoughts on how to best approach this for existing shareholders.

My free cash flow model indicates that Honeywell is trading for a marginal discount, roughly 3%, with an implied fair value of $221. The stock had a rough start to the year but has seen some strong momentum following the post-April tariff rebound. Some of that momentum has faded recently, with the stock once more dipping below its implied fair value.

My long-term return estimate for Honeywell stands at 10.48%; this is a 3-5 year estimated CAGR. It is comprised of the 2.1% forward dividend yield, a 0.59% return to fair value factor, and 7.79% projected earnings growth for the business.

There are a few ways existing shareholders can approach the upcoming changes for Honeywell. The company is attractively valued today with pretty good potential upside, suggesting that investors should hold onto their positions and see this breakup through. Each of the three new companies that will be spun off in the coming 18 months has the potential to bring additional upside through independent valuations and historically more attractive multiples in their respective business lines. Shareholders will have the flexibility to decide which of these new companies to hold, based on individual investment goals.

I believe each new company has the potential to grow into a great business with growth prospects. Ultimately, we’ll need to wait for the separations to be completed and evaluate each business on its own to gain a clear view of their individual outlooks.

I do not own any shares of Honeywell at the moment and have no intentions to initiate a position. However, if I were a shareholder, I would retain my shares through the breakup, as in my opinion the potential upside outweighs the downside.

data.title: Canada Unemployment Rate Surges To 7.1% In August
data.description:
The unemployment rate in Canada has risen to 7.1% in August, the highest level since May 2016 outside of the pandemic era, Statistics Canada (StatCan) reported Friday.

The surge in the jobless rate was driven by a 66,000 drop in employment in August, which followed a 41,000 decline in July. The labor force also shrank in the last two months, bringing the labor force participation rate down by 0.3 percentage points since June to 65.1%.

Full-time employment accounted for 6,000 of the total, while decline in part-time positions represented the majority of the drop at 59,700. Outside of the pandemic era, August marked the first time since Nov 2019 that job losses occurred in both full-time and part-time employment at the same month.

Professional, scientific and technical services (-26,100), transportation and warehousing (-22,700), manufacturing (-19,200) as well as education (-18,400) sectors all cut a substantial number of jobs in the month. These job losses were only partially offset by the solid employment growth in construction (17,100) and accommodation and food services (9,200) industries.

This job report reveals an apparent weakness in the Canadian labor market that will likely drag down consumption demand—one of the few remaining bright spots in Q2 GDP report—impacting the third quarter’s economic growth.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: UiPath: Proving Agentic AI Proficiency And Staging A Comeback
data.description:
Investing in the AI era has been incredibly tough this year. Almost every single tech company is claiming proficiency in deploying AI solutions, but there appears to be a clear divide between the stocks that investors have deemed as winners and those that have been marked as losers. On the one hand, there are mega-caps like Palantir (PLTR) that seemingly only move in one direction: up. On the other hand, other stocks like C3.ai (AI) and even cloud titans like Salesforce (CRM) have been tossed into the penalty box.

UiPath (NYSE:PATH), in my view, is attempting to make a crossover into the ascendant territory. The company just reported Q2 results that showcased a sharp acceleration in revenue growth and a healthy boost to full-year guidance, signaling increased adoption for its agentic AI-focused products.

I last wrote a bullish article on UiPath in July, when the stock was trading higher at ~$12 per share. Since then, UiPath has seen soaring growth while also padding its margin profile and bolstering its free cash flow story. Its ARR has continued to rise, adding to the durability of its revenue base. As such, I'm reiterating my "Buy" opinion on the stock.

To me, these are the core reasons to be long on UiPath:

  • UiPath has long focused on agentic AI, which enjoys secular tailwinds. Almost every software company has released some kind of AI tool, but most feature copilot or "intelligent recommendations" that don't offer true automation or labor-saving benefits. UiPath's agentic innovations, meanwhile, reach across every industry and help automate human tasks.
  • Industry recognition. UiPath is classified as one of the top Leaders by the Gartner Magic Quadrant, one of the most influential software industry reviewers.
  • Large and growing ARR base. UiPath's ARR (annualized recurring revenue) is substantially larger than its current-year revenue guidance, indicating a business composed of highly predictable and recurring revenue streams with room for upside. The excess of ARR to guidance also makes it likely for UiPath to keep issuing beat-and-raise quarters.
  • Economies of scale. UiPath is also hitting pro forma operating margins in the mid-teens, which is easy for a company with best-in-class margins in the mid-80%s.
  • Substantial cash reserves. The company's >$1 billion of cash represents nearly a quarter of the company's current market value, and gives UiPath plenty of financial flexibility to chase growth initiatives.

Despite these strengths, we find that UiPath still trades incredibly cheaply, especially for a stock buoyed by AI tailwinds. At current share prices in the mid-$11s, UiPath trades at a market cap of $6.12 billion. After we net off the $1.45 billion of cash (free of debt) on the company's latest balance sheet, UiPath's resulting enterprise value is $4.67 billion.

Meanwhile, UiPath has raised its current-year FY26 (the fiscal year ending in January 2026) revenue outlook to $1.571-$1.576 billion, representing 10% y/y growth, alongside a ~21.6% operating margin for the year. This is substantially higher than the 7% growth and ~18% margin that UiPath originally signaled in its prior guidance.

If we conservatively assume an FCF margin of 23%, flat to FY25 (and consistent with 1H'26 margins, though pro forma operating margins are expected to rise ~5 points this year), FCF would be $362 million.

For next year, FY26, Street estimates are pointing to $1.71 billion in revenue (+8% y/y), and a conservative 23% FCF margin assumption on that revenue profile would indicate $393 million in FCF. This positions UiPath's valuation multiples at:

  • 3.0x EV/FY26 revenue and 2.7x EV/FY27 revenue
  • 12.9x EV/FY26 FCF and 11.9x EV/FY27 FCF

These are incredibly cheap valuation multiples for an AI stock that is seeing revenue growth accelerate, has a large measure of safety in its ARR base, and is seeing soaring operating margins. Stay long here and ride the post-earnings momentum higher.

Q2 download

Let's now go through UiPath's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

UiPath's revenue grew 14% y/y to $361.7 million, blasting past Wall Street's expectations of $347.3 million (+10% y/y) by a four-point margin. UiPath's revenue growth also accelerated 8 points versus 6% y/y growth in Q1, which in turn improved versus 4% y/y growth in Q1.

UiPath's accelerating growth rates are proof of the market size for its agentic AI and automation products. But it isn't just secular tailwinds driving the improvements: UiPath notes as well that it has been modifying its sales organization, incorporating tighter pipeline reviews to generate higher-quality leads and improve closing rates. Per CEO Daniel Dines' remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

Importantly, our AI and Agentic solutions are helping us win deals and increase deal sizes faster than traditional automation engagements and now represent a growing share of commercial activity [...]

This momentum reflects the work we've completed to elevate execution. Over the past year, we rebuilt our go-to-market for scale, stabilizing our structure, adding sales capacity and specialists and instituting value-based plays with tighter pipeline inspection. We are seeing the impact: higher-quality pipeline, more predictable forecasting, better customer adoption and implementation and larger multi-solution opportunities, especially where Agentic and IDP land on top of our RPA and API foundation. You can see this execution showing up in the field. We continue to acquire high-quality new logos.

One of the best measures of UiPath's growth is its continued expansion of ARR. In Q2, the company added $30 million in net-new ARR, ending the quarter at $1.72 billion in ARR, up 11% y/y. On top of this, net retention rates are clocking in at 108%, indicating that within this current install base alone, UiPath is trending at ~8% net expansion.

This is a very bullish indicator, especially against very conservative Wall Street estimates for FY27. Just midway into FY26, the company's $1.72 billion in ARR is already greater than Street expectations for FY27 revenue, and that's not even counting the ~8% net expansion that we should expect to see from this existing customer base. In my view, as long as we don't see any sudden spike in churn or slowdown in new deal activity, UiPath should be able to comfortably execute healthy beat-and-raise quarters ahead.

Nor is UiPath ignoring profitability. As its ARR flywheel continues to give the company the benefits of built-in revenue growth without spending substantial incremental sales dollars, the company has capitalized on its 84% pro forma gross margin to generate substantial economies of scale.

In fact, UiPath actually achieved an acceleration to double-digit revenue growth while cutting nominal sales and marketing expenses (on a pro forma basis, excluding stock comp) by 5% y/y to $140.9 million. The company made even deeper cuts in general and administrative spending, down 11% y/y to $40.1 million. UiPath is showing by example the benefits of agentic AI in reducing headcount—unlike many other companies that tout AI automation benefits and yet are doing little to rein in headcount growth (ServiceNow (NOW) comes to mind).

As a result of these efficiency moves, UiPath's pro forma operating margins leaped 15 points y/y to 17%, and the company's guidance for ~22% full-year margins indicates further improvement ahead.

Key takeaways

Agentic AI deal momentum, healthy ARR growth, operating margin expansion driven by the company's own automation initiatives, and a very attractive valuation against revenue/FCF on top of rich cash reserves: There's a lot to like about UiPath exiting the Q2 earnings season. Continue to pay attention to the fact that ARR is well above current-year and forward-year estimates, which is a telling signal that UiPath should continue to blaze past Street expectations. Stay long here.

data.title: Is The Stock Market Overbought?
data.description:
The short answer: Yes, it appears to be. The somewhat longer answer: Yes, but (you knew this was coming) a number of indicators have been advising no less over the summer, and yet the market has continued to climb. Short-term timing decisions, in other words, remain as fraught as ever. But let’s press on anyway and consider some of the clues that imply the market is toppy.

Let’s start by noting that the trend remains red hot. The SPDR S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:SPY), a proxy for US equities, continues to exhibit a strong upside trend. As such, the case for arguing the opposite relies on a hefty dose of contrarian thinking. As such, it’s still reasonable to wait for signs of downside momentum before going too far in engaging in hedging.

Yet it’s also clear that market valuation is lofty. The Shiller PE, for instance, shows that the market has been valued at a higher level only once since the late 1800s. That alone doesn’t mean that a correction is imminent, but it’s a reminder that the margin for error is wafer thin re: negative surprises.

Analysts have been making similar points this summer, such as this July 12 headline: “The stock market now is clearly overbought – but overbought does not mean ‘sell’”.

What’s the bottom line? That depends on the investor, along with a host of other factors, such as time horizon, age, risk tolerance, etc. Where you fall along this multi-factor spectrum will influence your decision on how or if to adjust your portfolio, and so generic recommendations definitely do not apply here. A 25-year-old investor with a decades-long horizon can reasonably decide to ignore warning signs. Someone at or in retirement, by contrast, may want to think differently.

Meantime, a metric I monitor for gauging market sentiment tells me that the near-term outlook for the market appears overbought, based on this twist of estimating overbought/oversold conditions for the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). According to this indicator, the market is once again in elevated terrain that implies caution for the near-term outlook.

History suggests, although in no way guarantees, that as the Overbought-Oversold Indicator approaches and exceeds +1, the risk-reward prospects are skewed to elevated to the downside for stocks. Although the forecasting record is far from perfect, it’s not terribly surprising that as the indicator is near or above, corrections have been known to follow.

The current reading is 0.86, which is still modestly below +1, and so presumably the bull still has room to run. But if this indicator rises further, investors should consider what could keep the uptrend bubbling. Tech earnings? Economic resilience? Interest rate cuts?

Meanwhile, here’s my take: To the extent that the SPY’s Overbought-Oversold Indicator holds steady or rises from current levels, the market will be increasingly range-bound until/if a new catalyst reignites bullish sentiment.

The market has already priced in a favorable outlook from artificial intelligence and other tech-related drivers that are skewing S&P 500 earnings to the upside. The question, always: What might the incoming news bring, and will that be enough to justify higher valuations and prices?

No one knows, of course, but this much is clear: there’s relatively little room for disappointment at this stage. That alone shouldn’t determine your asset allocation, but it should at least inspire a discussion of what you think you know and what you could be overlooking.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
